dealSummary: count has hit its highest quarterly level this decade, up 80% year-over-year. Find out more • Two loans in Blackstone's giant private credit fund stopped making interest payments—one to software company Medallia, one to dental chain Affordable Care—causing the fund's non-performing debt ratio to rise from 0.6% to 2.4%. Read more OpenAI loosens Microsoft's grip ahead of IPO push NurPhoto/Getty Images By Jacob Robbins, Technology Reporter OpenAI and Microsoft have restructured their partnership again, shedding the last exclusivity deal with Azure, Microsoft's cloud computing unit. The new agreement gives OpenAI flexibility and leverage as it moves toward a potential IPO this year. "The greater predictability in the amended agreement strengthens our joint ability to build and operate AI platforms at scale while providing both companies the flexibility to pursue new opportunities," the companies said in a joint statement. Under the revised partnership, OpenAI can now sell API access to its models through any cloud provider. Until now, the company was limited to hosting its API products developed with third parties on Azure. Now it can offer them across competing platforms like Amazon Web Services or Google Cloud. The agreement still stipulates that OpenAI will launch its products first on Azure, but also states that Microsoft can opt to forgo that exclusivity if it so chooses or doesn't have the capacity. Microsoft's revenue share agreement with OpenAI is also ending. In the past, Microsoft paid OpenAI when users accessed models through Azure. OpenAI will continue to share its revenue with Microsoft, but only through a set date in 2030 and subject to a "total cap." This aspect of the agreement was originally supposed to be in place until OpenAI achieved true "artificial general intelligence." The agreement gives OpenAI more leverage to compete with Anthropic, its chief competitor, which it has accused of inflating its revenue, according to Harrison Rolfes, PitchBook's senior research analyst focusing on the big three large language model providers."OpenAI removed all the overhangs Anthropic was built without," he said. "OpenAI is definitely racing to look more like Anthropic. An IPO seems more likely, but they still have to tighten loose ends." Avoca, the developer of an AI platform for service businesses, secured a Series B led by Meritech and General Catalyst at a $1 billion valuation.

dealSummary: Medical device startup Nervonik raised a $52.5 million Series B led by Amzak Health.
Fathom Therapeutics, an AI drug discovery startup, secured a $47 million Series A led by Sutter Hill Ventures.
ComfyUI, the developer of a generative AI workflow platform, raised a $30 million round led by Craft Ventures at a $500 million valuation.
SquareMind, a Paris-based developer of a skin cancer detection robot, secured $18 million led by Bpifrance's Deeptech 2030 fund and Sonder Capital, Maddyness reported.
